translation: By those [angels] lined up in rows
commentary:
وَٱلصَّـٰٓفَّـٰتِ صَفًّا

The surah opens with an oath: "By those ranged in ranks." The صَّـٰٓفَّـٰت refers to beings standing in organized rows, and the dominant scholarly opinion identifies these as angels. Some scholars have suggested they could be winds, but the angelic interpretation is most strongly supported, particularly because later in this very surah, the angels speak about themselves using the masculine form: إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلصَّٰٓفُّونَ ("Indeed, we are those who stand in ranks").

Allah ﷻ uses the feminine plural form الصَّـٰٓفَّـٰت to indicate small groups or divisions. This grammatical choice paints a picture of a vast angelic army organized into sections, each section standing in precise rows. Imagine a military formation where different battalions are stationed in their designated positions—some angels have two wings, others three, others four, as mentioned elsewhere in the Quran. Each division stands ready, awaiting commands from their Lord, positioned like aircraft at a military base prepared for immediate deployment.

The word صَفًّا emphasizes the organization and discipline of these ranks—row upon row upon row, stretching beyond human comprehension. These are the soldiers of Allah ﷻ, standing at attention, prepared to execute His will at any moment.

translation: And those who drive [the clouds]
commentary:
فَٱلزَّٰجِرَٰتِ زَجْرًا

"Then by those who drive forcefully." This continues describing the same angels, now highlighting a different aspect of their mission. The الزَّٰجِرَٰتِ are those who scold or rebuke with tremendous force. This isn't a gentle reminder—زَجْرًا indicates a powerful, overwhelming scolding.

We've seen this concept before in the previous surah, where it was described as الصَّيْحَة—a devastating scream or shout. The angels deliver this rebuke with such intensity that the very sound can destroy entire towns. These are the angels of punishment, the executors of divine justice, whose voices carry the weight of Allah's ﷻ judgment. When they arrive to deliver their scolding, it is final and absolute.

translation: And those who recite the message
commentary:
فَٱلتَّـٰلِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا

"Then by those who recite the message." Here we see yet another battalion of angels—التَّـٰلِيَـٰتِ—those who recite and deliver revelation. These angels come bearing ذِكْرًا, the remembrance, the revelation from Allah ﷻ.

When revelation descends, it isn't carried by a single angel alone. An entire battalion of angels surrounds and protects Jibril (AS) as he brings the divine message to the prophets. These angels form a protective convoy for the most precious cargo in existence—the word of Allah ﷻ. They recite this remembrance to humanity, delivering spiritual guidance and divine instruction.

The progression in these three ayahs is remarkable: angels standing in ranks, angels delivering punishment, and angels bringing mercy through revelation. This demonstrates the comprehensive nature of angelic duties—they are both agents of justice and bearers of guidance.

translation: Indeed, your God is One
commentary:
إِنَّ إِلَـٰهَكُمْ لَوَٰحِدٌ

After these powerful oaths, we arrive at the statement they emphasize: "Indeed, your God is One." This is the central message, delivered with absolute certainty through the emphatic إِنَّ and the emphatic لَـ.

Why does Allah ﷻ use these angelic descriptions to establish His oneness? Because in pre-Islamic Arabia, one of the most widespread forms of shirk was the worship of angels as daughters of God, attributing to them independent divine powers. Allah ﷻ dismantles this belief by making it clear: these angels you worship are merely My standing army. They follow My commands. They have no independent power. The same angels you mistakenly worship are the ones I send to destroy those who commit shirk, and they are the same angels who bring revelation teaching you about My oneness.

The word إِلَـٰهَكُمْ—"your God"—emphasizes both deity and master, the One deserving worship and obedience. And He is وَٰحِدٌ—absolutely, uniquely One.

This is also unique among these five oath-based surahs. While the others typically conclude their oaths with statements about the Day of Judgment, As-Saffat uniquely uses its opening oaths to establish tawhid, the oneness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: Lord of the heavens and the earth and that between them and Lord of the sunrises
commentary:
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ

"Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and Lord of the sunrises." The description of Allah's ﷻ dominion continues and expands. He is رَبُّ—the Master, Sustainer, and Controller—of all creation: the heavens in their vastness, the earth beneath our feet, and everything that exists between them.

Then comes a fascinating phrase: وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ—"and Lord of the easts" or "Lord of the sunrises." Why mention the east (المَشَـٰرِق) specifically and not the west? Because every sunrise becomes someone else's sunset. Every east eventually becomes a west. The mention of one encompasses the other.

But there's a deeper rhetorical purpose. The east represents the beginning of light, the dawn breaking through darkness. The west represents the end of light, the approach of night. The angels bring revelation, which is spiritual light, spiritual guidance. This imagery suggests that after a long night of spiritual darkness, a new dawn has arrived in Arabia. The revelation brought by these angels is like the first rays of morning sun breaking through 600 years of darkness—the period between Prophet Isa (AS) and Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

This powerful imagery of light emerging from darkness will come full circle later in Surah Al-Muddaththir, where this contrast between the long night of ignorance and the morning of guidance is made even more explicit.

translation: Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven with an adornment of stars
commentary:
إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِزِينَةٍ ٱلْكَوَاكِبِ

"Indeed, We have adorned the lowest heaven with an adornment of stars." Allah ﷻ now describes how He has decorated the first sky—the one closest to us—with زِينَةٍ, beauty and adornment. The كَوَاكِبِ can refer to both stars and planets, those celestial bodies that light up our night sky.

But this beauty serves a purpose beyond aesthetics. These celestial bodies aren't merely decorative—they're part of a sophisticated defense system. The jinn are confined to this lowest heaven; they cannot ascend beyond the first sky to the higher realms where angels dwell and where divine knowledge resides. When angels descend with revelation or with commands for punishment, they must pass through this first heaven, and the jinn attempt to intercept them.

The stage is being set for an epic cosmic conflict, and these stars play a crucial role in the divine security system protecting sacred knowledge from those who would steal it.

translation: And as protection against every rebellious devil
commentary:
وَحِفْظًا مِّن كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّارِدٍ

"And as protection from every rebellious devil." This ayah explicitly states the defensive purpose of the previous ayah's beautification. The lowest heaven has been fortified—وَحِفْظًا, as a protection, a guardianship—against كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّارِدٍ, every rebellious, obstinate devil.

The word مَّارِدٍ describes a shaytaan who is extreme in evil, persistent in rebellion, and determined in disobedience. These devils know they are excluded from divine knowledge, yet their defiance drives them to attempt to steal it anyway. The jinn do not have access to knowledge of the unseen. When they sense that angels are descending—whether with revelation or with punishment—they race to try to catch up and eavesdrop.

Picture the scene from an old Western film: a train racing across the landscape while bandits on horseback chase alongside, trying desperately to board it. This is the image of the jinn pursuing the angels, racing through the cosmos, trying to intercept divine communication. But Allah ﷻ has installed protective measures in this lowest heaven specifically to prevent their success.

translation: [So] they may not listen to the exalted assembly [of angels] and are pelted from every side
commentary:
لَّا يَسَّمَّعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِن كُلِّ جَانِبٍ

"They cannot listen to the exalted assembly, and they are pelted from every side." The word لَّا يَسَّمَّعُونَ emphasizes intense effort in trying to listen. It's not casual hearing—it's straining, attempting with all their might to catch even a fragment of what the angels are communicating.

The ٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (al-mala'il-a'la)—the exalted assembly, the highest chiefs—refers to the gatherings of the most elevated angels, those closest to the divine throne, where matters of cosmic importance are discussed and decreed. The jinn desperately want access to this information, but they are blocked.

وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِن كُلِّ جَانِبٍ—"and they are pelted from every side." The moment they attempt to approach, they are bombarded. Missiles are launched at them from every direction. This isn't a small skirmish—it's a full-scale defensive operation. Anti-aircraft fire, except it's anti-jinn fire, comes at them from multiple angles simultaneously.

An intergalactic battle is taking place in the heavens above us. While we go about our daily lives on earth, there is an ongoing cosmic conflict between the forces of divine order and the forces of rebellious evil, between angels executing Allah's ﷻ commands and jinn trying to subvert His will.

translation: Repelled; and for them is a constant punishment
commentary:
دُحُورًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ وَاصِبٌ

"Expelled, and for them is a perpetual punishment." The word دُحُورًا means to be driven away with humiliation and disgrace. It's the kind of expulsion that happens when someone is thrown out of an establishment for terrible behavior—forceful, humiliating, absolute rejection.

This echoes the original expulsion of Iblis from Paradise. We read in Surah Al-Hijr that he would be مَدْحُورًا—driven out, disgraced. That same disgrace now applies to his followers, the rebellious jinn who dare to approach the sacred assembly of angels.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ وَاصِبٌ—"and for them is a perpetual punishment." The word وَاصِبٌ indicates punishment that is both intense and spread over a vast area. This isn't a confined torture in a small space—it's widespread suffering. The battlefield is enormous, and the punishment extends across it. These jinn face pain that is both severe in intensity and expansive in scope.

translation: Except one who snatches [some words] by theft, but they are pursued by a burning flame, piercing [in brightness]
commentary:
إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ ٱلْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ ثَاقِبٌ

"Except one who snatches a fragment, and is pursued by a piercing flame." Despite the overwhelming defensive fire, occasionally one of these jinn manages to slip through. إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ ٱلْخَطْفَةَ—"except the one who snatches a snatch"—indicates someone who grabs just a tiny fragment of information, a piece of a conversation, a snippet of knowledge.

But this momentary success doesn't last. فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ ثَاقِبٌ—"then a piercing meteor pursues him." The word أَتْبَعَ means to follow from behind, and شِهَابٌ ثَاقِبٌ describes a blazing, brilliant meteor—essentially a heat-seeking missile.

This projectile is ثَاقِبٌ, piercing, penetrating everything in its path. The word also suggests something red with fire, glowing with intense heat. This cosmic missile locks onto the fleeing jinn and—kablooey—obliterates him completely.

You would think the jinn would learn their lesson after seeing their comrades destroyed. But they don't, because despite their intelligence, they are ultimately foolish in their rebellion. They keep going back, keep trying to steal information, and keep getting incinerated. This is the endless cycle of defiance and destruction playing out in the heavens.

translation: Then inquire of them, [O Muhammad], "Are they a stronger [or more difficult] creation or those [others] We have created?" Indeed, We created men from sticky clay
commentary:
فَٱسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَم مَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّن طِينٍ لَّازِبٍۭ

After this intense cosmic scene—fires in the sky, intergalactic warfare, angels and jinn in conflict—Allah ﷻ suddenly shifts the focus to humanity with a humbling question.

فَٱسْتَفْتِهِمْ—"Then ask them." The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to pose a question to these arrogant disbelievers: أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَم مَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ —"Are they a stronger creation, or are those others We have created?" Are you humans more powerfully made than the angels who stand in ranks, than the jinn who race through the heavens, than the celestial bodies and the vast cosmos?

Then comes the humbling reality: إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّن طِينٍ لَّازِبٍۭ—"Indeed, We created them from sticky clay." Not just any clay—لَّازِبٍۭ means adhesive, clinging, swampy mud. This is the humblest origin imaginable.

The contrast is devastating. You've just been told about battles involving creatures of light (angels) and creatures of fire (jinn) waging war in the heavens. Meanwhile, you—human being—are made from sticky swamp mud. Who exactly do you think you are? What gives you the right to be arrogant? What makes you think you can challenge the Creator or doubt His message?

This echoes an earlier surah where we were told that we are created from نُطْفَةٍ, a drop of liquid. Now we're reminded we're from لَّازِبٍۭ, sticky mud. These reminders of our humble origins are meant to instill humility and perspective. In the face of cosmic battles and divine power, human arrogance is absurdly misplaced.

translation: But you wonder, while they mock
commentary:
بَلْ عَجِبْتَ وَيَسْخَرُونَ

"Rather, you are amazed while they mock." This ayah captures the Prophet's ﷺ emotional state in one reading, though there is another reading that makes Allah ﷻ the one who is amazed. In the reading بَلْ عَجِبْتَ, the Prophet ﷺ is the subject—you, Muhammad ﷺ, are astounded, shocked, bewildered by their response.

وَيَسْخَرُونَ—"while they mock and ridicule." You're explaining cosmic realities to them, revealing divine truths, describing the armies of Allah ﷻ and the fate that awaits the disbelievers, and their response is to laugh and make fun. How can they hear all of this and remain unmoved? How can they treat revelation as entertainment?

This is a frustration that every Muslim understands. You sit with a family member for hours, explaining Islam, answering their questions, showing them the beauty and truth of the message. You take them to an amazing khutbah about the Day of Judgment, with descriptions that should shake anyone's soul. And immediately afterward, they say, "Yeah, cool, but I'm going to this party tonight." 

The disconnect is maddening. How does the message not penetrate? How can someone hear about Paradise and Hell, about angels and divine justice, about life's purpose and the accountability we'll face, and then just shrug it off? Yet this is exactly what the Prophet ﷺ faced, and it's what believers continue to face when trying to guide those they love.

translation: And when they are reminded, they remember not
commentary:
وَإِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ لَا يَذْكُرُونَ

"And when they are reminded, they do not take heed." The word ذُكِّرُوا۟ indicates a strong, powerful reminder—not a casual mention but a serious, forceful warning. Yet لَا يَذْكُرُونَ—they don't even try to remember a little bit. They don't make the slightest effort to reflect or retain the message.

This isn't about forgetting over time. This is about immediate, willful rejection. The reminder comes with force and clarity, and they consciously choose not to let it register at all. They hear the words but refuse to process their meaning or implications. Their hearts and minds are closed before the message even has a chance to enter.

translation: And when they see a sign, they ridicule
commentary:
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ

"And when they see a sign, they ridicule." Even when confronted with an ءَايَةً—a miraculous sign, undeniable proof of divine power—their response is يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ. This intensive form means they actively seek ways to mock it, to turn it into a joke. They go out of their way to find something ridiculous about it.

When someone is determined not to believe, even miracles become comedy material. Instead of acknowledging the supernatural nature of what they're witnessing, instead of allowing themselves to be amazed or humbled, they twist it into mockery. They search for any angle that allows them to dismiss it, to turn something profound into something trivial.

This is the tragedy of a hardened heart: evidence doesn't soften it, and miracles don't crack it open. The problem isn't a lack of proof—it's a refusal to accept proof.

translation: And say, "This is not but obvious magic
commentary:
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

"And they said, 'This is nothing but obvious magic.'" After seeing clear signs, after witnessing miracles, their official verdict is سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ—obvious, clear magic. They categorize divine miracles as mere magic tricks, as deception and illusion.

This dismissal serves multiple purposes for them. First, it allows them to avoid confronting the truth—if it's just magic, they don't have to change. Second, it maintains their social standing—they can't admit they were wrong or that an orphan from their own city has been chosen for prophethood. Third, it protects their economic interests and way of life, which would be threatened by accepting Islam.

But notice the irony: they call it سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ, "obvious magic." If it were obvious magic, wouldn't they be able to replicate it or expose its mechanism? Their own description undermines their accusation. What they're really saying is, "This is obviously beyond what we can explain, but we refuse to acknowledge its divine origin, so we'll call it magic instead."

## Ayah 16-17

أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ - أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ

"When we die and become dust and bones, will we really be resurrected? And our forefathers as well?" Here we encounter their fundamental objection to the message: disbelief in resurrection.

The progression they describe is telling—أَءِذَا مِتْنَا, when we die, وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا, and we become dust. Most of the body dissolves into the earth, returns to soil. وَعِظَـٰمًا—and bones. The bones are mentioned specifically because they're the last thing to go, the final remnant of a person's physical existence. The flesh, the organs, the blood—all absorbed by the earth. But the bones remain longer.

Their question drips with sarcasm: أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ—"We're really going to be raised up again? Seriously?" And then they extend the absurdity even further: أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ—"And our ancient forefathers too? Even those whose bones have completely disintegrated, who died so long ago that nothing remains?"

In their minds, this makes the resurrection even more ridiculous. It's one thing to claim that recently deceased people might be brought back, but people who died centuries ago? Whose remains have completely vanished? That, to them, is beyond absurd.

But they fail to understand that for Allah ﷻ, who created the heavens and the earth, who maintains armies of angels, who orchestrates cosmic battles, who created humans from mud in the first place—recreating them from dust is effortless. The One who initiated creation can certainly repeat it.

translation: When we have died and become dust and bones, are we indeed to be resurrected
And our forefathers [as well]
Say, "Yes, and you will be [rendered] contemptible
commentary:
قُلْ نَعَمْ وَأَنتُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ

The response to their sarcastic question is direct and devastating: قُلْ نَعَمْ—"Say: Yes." No elaborate explanation, no philosophical argument, just a straightforward confirmation. Yes, you will be resurrected. Yes, your forefathers will be brought back. Yes, all of it is absolutely true.

But then comes the crushing addition: وَأَنتُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ—"and you will be humiliated." The word دَٰخِرُونَ indicates complete humiliation, submission, and disgrace. All the arrogance you display now, all the mockery, all the sarcasm—it will be gone. You will be utterly humbled and brought low.

Your disbelief stems from pride and stubbornness, from an attitude that you're too intelligent or sophisticated to believe in resurrection. So you will be specifically humiliated for that very attitude. The irony is perfect: you thought you were too smart to believe, but your disbelief was actually the height of foolishness, and that foolishness will be exposed for everyone to see.

translation: It will be only one shout, and at once they will be observing
commentary:
فَإِنَّمَا هِىَ زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَإِذَا هُمْ يَنظُرُونَ

"Then it will be only one shout, and at once they will be looking." Remember earlier in the surah, the angels were described as الزَّٰجِرَٰتِ زَجْرًا, those who drive with a powerful scolding. Now that same concept returns: زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ—a single, solitary shout.

That's all it will take. One cry from an angel, one blast, one command, and فَإِذَا هُمْ يَنظُرُونَ—suddenly, immediately, they will be looking, staring, observing their new reality. No gradual awakening, no slow transition—one instant they're dead and dissolved in their graves, the next instant they're standing, fully conscious, facing the reality they denied.

The speed emphasizes how effortless it is for Allah ﷻ. They thought resurrection was impossible, that it would require some massive, complicated process. But it happens in an instant, with a single sound.

translation: They will say, "O woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ

"And they will say, 'Woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense.'" The moment they're resurrected and see where they are, their first words are يَـٰوَيْلَنَا—"O our destruction! Oh no! What terrible thing has befallen us!"

They immediately recognize هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ—"This is the Day of Judgment," the Day of Recompense, the Day of Religion, the day when accounts are settled. Everything they mocked, everything they dismissed, everything they called impossible—it's all happening exactly as they were warned.

Notice that they don't even need to be told where they are or what's happening. The reality is so obvious, so overwhelming, that recognition is instant. This is the day. The day they denied. The day they laughed about. And now they're living it.

translation: [They will be told], "This is the Day of Judgement which you used to deny
commentary:
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ

The angels or Allah ﷻ responds to their realization: "This is the Day of Decision which you used to deny." The يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ is the Day of Separation, the Day of Final Decision, when truth is separated from falsehood, believers from disbelievers, the righteous from the wicked.

ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ—"which you used to deny." The word تُكَذِّبُونَ means to repeatedly call something a lie, to persistently reject it. This wasn't a one-time doubt or a momentary skepticism—this was continuous, deliberate, stubborn denial.

Remember all those times you called it magic? All those times you mocked the believers? All those times you laughed at warnings about this day? Well, here it is. You're standing in it. Your denial didn't make it less real; it only made your situation worse.

## Ayah 22-23

۞ ٱحْشُرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَأَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ - مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَٱهْدُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

Now the command is issued to the angels—those same angels we were introduced to at the beginning of the surah, standing in ranks, awaiting orders. ٱحْشُرُوا۟—"Gather together," round up ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟, those who did wrong, those who transgressed, those who were unjust to themselves and others.

وَأَزْوَٰجَهُمْ—"and their spouses" or more accurately, "their kinds," their class, their social circle, their elite club. The word أَزْوَٰج doesn't just mean marital partners here; it means their associates, their partners in crime, their fellow members of the corruption club. These people had their country clubs, their secret societies, their elite gatherings where they reinforced each other's disbelief and planned their opposition to the truth.

There's another interpretation: أَزْوَٰجَهُمْ could refer to those similar to them in sin. Gather the tyrants with other tyrants, the mockers with other mockers, the deniers with other deniers. Birds of a feather, now gathered together for judgment.

وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ—"and whatever they used to worship besides Allah." Bring their idols, their false gods, everything they elevated above or alongside Allah ﷻ. These objects of worship will be brought as evidence against them and as witnesses to their crimes.

فَٱهْدُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ—"then guide them to the path of the Hellfire." The bitter irony here is devastating. They refused to be guided to the صِرَٰطِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ, the straight path that leads to Allah's ﷻ pleasure and Paradise. Now they will be "guided"—forcefully led, marched—to a different path: the path to الْجَحِيمِ, the Hellfire.

And notice the description of this guidance: as you walk along this path, the roar of the fire gets louder and louder. You're not suddenly thrown into Hell; you're marched toward it, and with every step, the sound of the flames intensifies. The heat increases. The reality of what awaits becomes more and more terrifying. But there's no escape, no turning back. The path only goes one direction.

translation: [The angels will be ordered], "Gather those who committed wrong, their kinds, and what they used to worship
Other than Allah, and guide them to the path of Hellfire
And stop them; indeed, they are to be questioned
commentary:
وَقِفُوهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُم مَّسْـُٔولُونَ

"And stop them, for indeed they are to be questioned." Before they reach their final destination, there's a pause. وَقِفُوهُمْ (waqifuhum)—"Stop them, make them stand right here."

This appears to contradict another ayah in the Quran: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُسْـَٔلُ عَن ذَنۢبِهِۦٓ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَآنٌّ (So on that Day none will be asked about his sin, neither human nor jinn) (55:39). How can the Quran say they won't be questioned and also say they will be questioned?

The answer lies in understanding the stages of the Day of Judgment. In the initial moments after resurrection—when everyone is emerging from their graves and the first blast has sounded—there will be no need to ask who the criminals are. It will be obvious from people's faces, from their condition, from the way they're drowning in their own sweat while others stand in shade. يُعْرَفُ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ (The criminals will be recognized by their marks) (55:41).

But after everyone has stood and the chaos has settled, then comes the interrogation. That's when we reach this ayah: وَقِفُوهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُم مَّسْـُٔولُونَ—"Stop them, for they are to be questioned." Now it's time for the detailed questioning, the accountability, the examination of every deed and every intention.

translation: [They will be asked], "What is [wrong] with you? Why do you not help each other
commentary:
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنَاصَرُونَ

The questioning begins with dripping sarcasm: "What is wrong with you? Why don't you help each other?" مَا لَكُمْ—"What's the matter with you?" لَا تَنَاصَرُونَ—"You're not helping one another?"

Picture this scene: all these prisoners in their hellfire jumpsuits, standing in formation. The billionaires, the trillionaires, the power brokers who used to run the world. The celebrities, the influencers, the elite who shaped public opinion. The gang members, the mafia bosses, the warlords who instilled fear in entire regions. All of them standing together, powerless.

And Allah ﷻ asks them with divine sarcasm: Where's your mutual support now? What happened to your networks? Your connections? Your backup? Your security teams? Your lawyers? Your PR people? Your bodyguards? You used to call for help and armies would mobilize. You used to make a phone call and problems would disappear. Where's all that power now?

translation: But they, that Day, are in surrender
commentary:
بَلْ هُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ

"Rather, they are this Day in complete submission." The word مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ means to surrender completely, to give up, to submit without resistance. All their defiance, all their arrogance, all their power—gone.

These are the same people who refused to submit to Allah ﷻ in their lives. They rejected His commands, mocked His messengers, and proudly declared their independence from divine authority. Now they're مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ—completely submitted, utterly surrendered, totally compliant—but it's too late for this submission to benefit them.

The surrender that would have brought them honor and Paradise in the worldly life is now happening in humiliation, and it leads only to punishment. They're submitting not out of faith but out of sheer helplessness. There's literally nothing else they can do.

translation: And they will approach one another blaming each other
commentary:
وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

"And they will approach one another, questioning each other." After standing in submission before divine judgment, now they turn to each other. وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—some of them face others, يَتَسَآءَلُونَ—asking each other questions, interrogating one another.

This is where the blame game begins. When people face consequences together, they often turn on each other. The unity they had in sin dissolves in the face of punishment. Now it's time to point fingers, to assign blame, to accuse and defend.

translation: They will say, "Indeed, you used to come at us from the right
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ تَأْتُونَنَا عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ

The followers address their leaders: "Indeed, you used to come to us from the right." The phrase عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ is rich with meaning. Literally, it means "from the right," but in Arabic culture, اليمين carries connotations of power, authority, and strength. It's the position of influence and respect.

They're saying: "You came to us from a position of power. You had status, influence, authority. When you greeted us, we took you seriously because of who you were—wealthy, powerful, respected. You gave us advice and we listened because you had credentials, because you were in positions of leadership, because society looked up to you."

"You told us religion was backwards. You said the Messenger was crazy. You convinced us that the resurrection was a myth and that we should focus on enjoying this life. And we believed you because of your position, because you came from the right—from a place of power and authority."

translation: The oppressors will say, "Rather, you [yourselves] were not believers
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ بَل لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ

The leaders respond with a devastating deflection: "Rather, you yourselves were not believers." قَالُوا۟—they said, بَل—no, rather, actually, لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ—you were not believers yourselves.

"Don't blame us. We didn't force belief out of you. You already didn't believe. We didn't have to work that hard to convince you. Your hearts were already inclined toward disbelief. You were already looking for excuses not to believe. We just gave you what you wanted to hear. We just provided justification for what you already wanted to do."

This is the first line of defense: complete denial of responsibility. "Your disbelief was your own. We didn't create it; we just enabled it."

translation: And we had over you no authority, but you were a transgressing people
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ لَنَا عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ ۖ بَلْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا طَـٰغِينَ

They continue their defense: "And we had no authority over you. Rather, you were a transgressing people." وَمَا كَانَ لَنَا عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ —"We didn't have any power over you, any control, any authority to force you to do anything."

"We didn't hold guns to your heads. We didn't threaten you. We didn't compel you. You had free will, and you chose to follow us. You could have investigated for yourselves. You could have listened to the Messenger. You could have read the revelation. But you chose not to."

بَلْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا طَـٰغِينَ—"Rather, you were a people who were transgressing." The word طَـٰغِينَ means rebellious, crossing boundaries, exceeding limits. "You were rebels at heart. You were naturally inclined to transgress. We didn't make you that way—you already were that way. Your transgression was your own. All we did was walk beside you on the path you had already chosen."

This exchange captures the tragedy of Hell: everyone blaming everyone else, everyone denying responsibility, everyone claiming they were just following or just leading but not really accountable. The unity they had in sin is completely shattered in punishment, replaced by accusations, finger-pointing, and desperate attempts to shift blame.

But ultimately, these arguments are meaningless. Both groups—leaders and followers, influencers and influenced—are standing in the same place, facing the same judgment, heading toward the same punishment. The blame game changes nothing about their destination.

translation: So the word of our Lord has come into effect upon us; indeed, we will taste [punishment]
commentary:
فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنَا قَوْلُ رَبِّنَآ ۖ إِنَّا لَذَآئِقُونَ

The word of our Master has already manifested upon us. We are truly going to be tasters of this punishment. The verdict has been passed.

In the previous surah, Al-Kafiroon, we learned that the verdict *may* be passed on the disbelievers. Now, in this passage, the verdict *has* passed. The messenger has been sent, the Quran has come down to warn those who are still alive, and now the decree against the disbelievers is being fulfilled. They acknowledge their fate: "The verdict has come to pass. We deserve it—both of us deserve it."

These criminals are speaking to one another in the Hereafter, essentially saying, "You were pretty bad on your own, yes, but I was an influential figure who misled you too. So now I'm here as well." The word of our Lord has become deserving upon us, and now we're constantly tasting this punishment. There's no point in arguing with each other anymore—it's a done deal.

translation: And we led you to deviation; indeed, we were deviators
commentary:
فَأَغْوَيْنَـٰكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غَـٰوِينَ

We misled you because we ourselves had always been lost. The word غَـٰوِينَ carries the meaning of being fundamentally astray. It's not that these misleaders guided their followers to something meaningful—they led them astray because they themselves were completely off the path. They're admitting, "We weren't guiding you toward truth. We were lost, and we took you down with us."

translation: So indeed they, that Day, will be sharing in the punishment
commentary:
فَإِنَّهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ

On that Day, those people are without doubt going to be partners in the punishment. They're going to be sharing in the torment. The misleaders and the misled, the influential and the influenced—all of them will be مُشْتَرِكُونَ, sharing equally in their fate.

translation: Indeed, that is how We deal with the criminals
commentary:
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

This is exactly how We deal with criminals. No doubt, this is in fact how We deal with those who commit crimes against truth and against themselves.

translation: Indeed they, when it was said to them, "There is no deity but Allah," were arrogant
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ

Those are the kinds of people who, whenever it was said to them "لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ"—there is no deity worthy of worship except Allah ﷻ—they would show pride. The word يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ captures their arrogance. They would try to show their superiority, to display their arrogance in response to the simplest and most profound truth.

translation: And were saying, "Are we to leave our gods for a mad poet
commentary:
وَيَقُولُونَ أَئِنَّا لَتَارِكُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتِنَا لِشَاعِرٍ مَّجْنُونٍۭ

And they would turn around and say, "Are we really going to abandon our gods for this insane poet?" This is their response to the call of truth. They dismiss the message entirely, claiming the messenger is nothing but a شَاعِرٍ مَّجْنُونٍ—a crazy poet.

Notice the connection to the end of the previous surah, where Allah ﷻ declared, "We did not teach him poetry." Yet here they are, calling the Prophet ﷺ a poet. They want to reduce the miraculous Quran to mere poetry so they can justify their rejection.

translation: Rather, the Prophet has come with the truth and confirmed the [previous] messengers
commentary:
بَلْ جَآءَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَصَدَّقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

Rather, he came with the truth and confirmed all the previous messengers. The word بَلْ is a powerful corrective—it means "No, actually, quite the opposite." He didn't come as a poet. He came with حَقّ—absolute truth. And not only that, he وَصَدَّقَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ —he confirmed and validated the truth of all the messengers who came before him. This is the role of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: to complete and confirm the message that has always been the same.

translation: Indeed, you [disbelievers] will be tasters of the painful punishment
commentary:
إِنَّكُمْ لَذَآئِقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْأَلِيمِ

You people are the continuous and constant and endless tasters of the painful punishment. The word لَذَآئِقُوا emphasizes ongoing, repeated tasting. This isn't a one-time experience—it's eternal and unceasing.

translation: And you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do
commentary:
وَمَا تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

And you are not being compensated with anything at all except what you did yourselves. This is perfect justice. No one is being wronged. The punishment matches exactly what they earned through their own choices and actions.

translation: But not the chosen servants of Allah
commentary:
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

Not so is the case for the slaves of Allah ﷻ who were granted sincerity. This ayah introduces a beautiful distinction. The word مُخْلَصِينَ is particularly significant. In Arabic, مُخْلِص is someone who is sincere on their own effort, while مُخْلَص is someone who has been granted sincerity as a divine gift.

You put in the work, you ask Allah ﷻ for sincerity, you try your best to do everything for His sake alone. But on top of your intentions and efforts, He purifies them in a way you're not capable of on your own. He grants you a sincerity you cannot achieve by yourself. These are the عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ (slaves of Allah ﷻ) who have been given, through divine intervention, a special purity of purpose.

translation: Those will have a provision determined
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَّعْلُومٌ

Those people are going to have known provisions, known sustenance. The word مَّعْلُومٌ tells us it's established, guaranteed, specified. Throughout the Quran, when Allah ﷻ describes Paradise and its blessings, He often calls the provisions رِزْقٌ مَّعْلُومٌ—it's known exactly what they're going to receive.

translation: Fruits; and they will be honored
commentary:
فَوَٰكِهُ ۖ وَهُم مُّكْرَمُونَ

Tasty fruits, and they are going to be honored. The blessings of Paradise aren't just material—there's honor, dignity, and respect. وَهُم مُّكْرَمُونَ—they are treated with utmost honor.

translation: In gardens of pleasure
commentary:
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

In the gardens of constant favor, constant luxury. Now we're getting a particular dimension of Paradise. In Surah Yaseen, we saw Paradise described as a place of constant activity. But what about those who, even in Paradise, might want to just relax? Even if activities don't exhaust you there, sometimes you just want to sit back and enjoy. Allah ﷻ says these are not just gardens of activity—these are جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ, gardens of softness, relaxation, and luxury.

translation: On thrones facing one another
commentary:
عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ

On lofty beds and couches, they're going to be facing one another. Imagine gardens with a massive, beautiful living room where you're sitting comfortably, and you can see everybody. There's now a gathering, a reunion. In this life, when you're at a party or gathering, you can only talk to so many people at once. It's limited—there's a huge crowd, but your interaction is restricted. But Allah ﷻ will arrange it so that everyone gets to see everyone else.

"Hey, you're here! Yeah, I didn't think you'd make it!" "Welcome! It's awesome to have you here too. How's it been? When did you arrive?" "Well, I died when I was eighty-two." "I died when I was forty." And then someone in the back says, "I've been here since the third century—I was a shaheed!" "Tell us your story!"

Everybody is facing everybody, talking, reminiscing. This is what friends do when they meet after a long time—they talk about old times.

translation: There will be circulated among them a cup [of wine] from a flowing spring
commentary:
يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍۭ

Servants are constantly running around with drinks, because when you're sitting on couches and talking, you need refreshments. The word يُطَافُ indicates circulation—they're going around continuously. And since everyone is relaxed on their couches, they shouldn't have to get up. "Hold on, I'm a little thirsty. Give me a minute. You want something?" No, no, everyone just stays where they are.

There are servants passing out كَأْسٍ with drinks that are مِّن مَّعِينٍ—from a flowing spring, clear and pure. They're passing them out continuously, and everyone is receiving drinks without having to move.

translation: White and delicious to the drinkers
commentary:
بَيْضَآءَ لَذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ

White, pure, and delicious for those who drink. The drink is بَيْضَآءَ—white, crystal clear. You can see right through it. And it's لَذَّةٍ—a word that captures both the beauty of its appearance and the pleasure of its taste.

Sometimes in this world, a drink looks appealing but when you taste it, you're disappointed. The color attracts you, but the taste doesn't match. This describes my experience with rose water, for example—I love strawberry milk, and the first time I saw rose water, I thought it would be similar. But halfway through the glass, I realized it was nothing like what I expected.

In Paradise, the appearance is enticing *and* the taste is pure. The color itself seems to have a taste. When something is described as "white in taste," it means the taste is going to be pure, fresh, unadulterated—exactly what you hoped for.

translation: No bad effect is there in it, nor from it will they be intoxicated
commentary:
لَا فِيهَا غَوْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ عَنْهَا يُنزَفُونَ

No غَوْلٌ in it, and they will not be depleted from it. The word غَوْلٌ carries multiple meanings—the closest equivalent in our context is a hangover, or the loss of mental faculties that comes from intoxication. When people get excessively drunk, they see things that aren't there, their faces seem to morph, they become delusional. غَوْلٌ is also used for evil spirits, possession, someone losing their mind, not making sense when they talk, becoming terrified, or getting nightmares.

These drinks in Paradise won't do any of that. And وَلَا هُمْ عَنْهَا يُنزَفُونَ—they won't be drained out. You won't hear, "Sorry sir, we've run out." There's no running dry. It also means you won't get a headache from the drinks. No brain freeze in Paradise—you can drink as fast as you want and nothing uncomfortable happens.

With alcoholic drinks in this world—alhamdulillah we haven't experienced this—but we know what happens to people: they throw up, get in car accidents, suffer massive headaches, experience hangovers. None of that will happen in Paradise.

translation: And with them will be women limiting [their] glances, with large, [beautiful] eyes
commentary:
وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ عِينٌ

And in their company are beautiful spouses described as قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ—those who lower their gaze. They're modest, they're shy. They don't want to make direct eye contact with their spouses out of bashfulness. And their eyes are described as عِينٌ—sparkly, clear, crystal-like eyes.

translation: As if they were [delicate] eggs, well-protected
commentary:
كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَّكْنُونٌ

As though they are hidden eggs. Now when you hear "hidden eggs," you might think, "My beautiful spouse in Paradise is like... a carton of eggs in the fridge?" But this is describing something entirely different and profound.

The word مَّكْنُونٌ comes from كِنّ—to be in the indent of a mountain. Picture a mountain climber ascending higher and higher until he reaches the peak, and there he discovers an eagle's nest with these pristine, beautiful eggs. It's a discovery—something nobody has ever seen before, something rare and precious.

بَيْضٌ مَّكْنُونٌ implies they are like a hidden treasure. When you see them, it's as if you've never seen anyone that beautiful before. And they're shy, they don't even make direct eye contact. قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ—they're modest in their gaze.

translation: And they will approach one another, inquiring of each other
commentary:
فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

So they go back out to the gathering, and they're facing one another again, asking each other questions. After spending time with their spouses in their private dwellings, they return to the social gathering. "Oh my God, I remember you! How have you been? How was Judgment Day for you?" "Oh, I was so scared at the beginning, but then when the angel gave me the book in my right hand, I was overwhelmed with relief!"

It's like the conversation people have after an exam. "How did it go for you?" "Oh man, when I got to question number five, I panicked!" "What did you get? What did you write?" There's this reunion happening. You haven't seen these people in so long—you died centuries ago, then came Judgment Day where you only cared about yourself, then you made it into Paradise. First you saw your own family, then your spouse, and now there's this gathering where you're starting to meet people you didn't expect.

People from New York, people from different cities, your cousins, the imam of the masjid, friends you made at a convention—everyone. "Are you here too? How are you? What happened?" They're meeting each other, asking questions, catching up. "What was it like for you?"

They're sitting, relaxing, just reminiscing about old times. That's what friends do when they meet after a long time—they talk about the past.

translation: A speaker among them will say, "Indeed, I had a companion [on earth]
commentary:
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ إِنِّى كَانَ لِى قَرِينٌ

One of them said, "I used to have this friend I always hung out with." The word قَرِينٌ actually means someone who comes right next to something, like one generation flows into another. "I had a friend I was always with. There was this person—we sat in every class together. We were inseparable. People used to think we were siblings. We hung out all the time."

translation: Who would say, 'Are you indeed of those who believe
commentary:
يَقُولُ أَءِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصَدِّقِينَ

Then he has a flashback. "Oh, that friend of mine... When I became Muslim—or when I really, truly became Muslim—he turned to me and said, 'Are you for real? Are you seriously confirming this truth? What's gotten into you?'"

In our time, this is someone saying, "What, are you for real, man? Prayer? Hell? Paradise? What are you talking about? Let's go, there's a party. Let's go." "No, I'm afraid of Hellfire." "Where did you get this from? What's wrong with you? What happened to you, man? Forget it, I'll just go without you. You're missing out."

translation: That when we have died and become dust and bones, we will indeed be recompensed
commentary:
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَدِينُونَ

"When we die and become dust and decayed bones, are we really going to be brought to justice? Are we going to stand before Allah ﷻ and be judged? مَدِينُونَ—brought before judgment, held accountable? Where are you getting this stuff from? You used to be so much fun to hang out with. We used to chill together. I don't know what's wrong with you now."

This was the last conversation he remembers having with his friend. After that exchange, they stopped hanging out. This friend went his way, the believer went his way. They got married, moved to different cities, life moved on. He doesn't remember that high school buddy, that college friend from back in the day.

But then in Paradise, the memory comes back. We're learning something profound here: in Paradise, you will remember everything about this life.

translation: He will say, "Would you [care to] look
commentary:
قَالَ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّطَّلِعُونَ

He says to everyone else sitting in the gathering, while drinks are being passed around, "Hey, would you guys like to find out what happened to him? Would you like to investigate and discover?" The word مُّطَّلِعُونَ means to climb up to discover something, like climbing a mountain to see what's on the other side.

Now, what have we learned about Paradise? You have whatever you wish for. And what wish did he just make? "I want to know what happened to my friend."

translation: And he will look and see him in the midst of the Hellfire
commentary:
فَٱطَّلَعَ فَرَءَاهُ فِى سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

So he looked, and Allah ﷻ opened a window for him, showing him a view into Hellfire. And he saw his friend فِى سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ—in the middle of the blazing fire. Deep in the thick of it. Remember the description of the path to the roaring fire, getting louder and louder? His friend is in the middle of the jaheem.

Keep in mind where this scene is happening: this person is in Paradise. They've received a view into Hell only because they requested it—"I want to see my friend." Even that wish is granted in Paradise.

translation: He will say, "By Allah, you almost ruined me
commentary:
قَالَ تَٱللَّهِ إِن كِدتَّ لَتُرْدِينِ

He looks at his friend and says, "تَٱللَّهِ—I swear by Allah ﷻ, you almost made me fall!" 

The phrase تَٱللَّهِ is never used except in cases of emergency, in intense situations. Normally in Arabic you say وَٱللَّهِ—"By Allah ﷻ, it's hot today," or "By Allah ﷻ, I don't know," or "By Allah ﷻ, I really dislike that." But تَٱللَّهِ is when you mean serious business, when it's truly intense and critical.

He doesn't even ask, "Are you okay? Can I do something for you? Can we get you some water?"—even though water is being passed around right there. The first thing that comes out of his mouth is, "I swear to God, you almost made me fall off the cliff too!"

Notice all the imagery of cliffs and mountains in these passages—climbing up the mountain, discovering on the mountain, dropping off a cliff, pushing someone off a cliff. He's saying, "Man, you almost got me pushed off the cliff. You almost dragged me down with you. I almost hung out with you long enough to adopt the same lifestyle you had. I almost did the haram things with you that you were up to. Oh my God, that was close—إِن كِدتَّ—you nearly succeeded in destroying me."

translation: If not for the favor of my Lord, I would have been of those brought in [to Hell]
commentary:
وَلَوْلَا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ

"Had it not been for the favor of my Master, I would have been among those who are made to stand." The word ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ refers to those who are brought forward, presented for evaluation and interrogation.

But wait—where is he? He's in Paradise. Has Judgment Day already passed? Yes. So what is he talking about?

He's reminiscing. "I remember when I was passing by, making my way to the gates of Paradise, and I saw some people who couldn't proceed because they were being interrogated." You know how at airport security there's an express line for some people, and then there's the regular line for everyone else? And you see some people just breeze through while millions are standing there waiting? This person had the VIP pass. As he was walking straight into Paradise, he glanced over and saw those enormous crowds—millions of people standing, waiting for their interrogation.

He's saying, "If it weren't for Allah's ﷻ favor, I would have been in that group, because that's where people like my friend end up."

translation: Then, are we not to die
commentary:
أَفَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ

Now he's reliving the events of Judgment Day just because he caught a glimpse of Hellfire. Then the window closes—he doesn't want to look anymore. His wish has been granted. The window is closed.

Where is he again? Paradise.

But here's what's remarkable: now that the window is closed and he looks at Paradise again, it's like he's seeing it for the first time. It's as if he was never there before. Was he already there? Was he already hanging out with everyone? Has Judgment passed? Has everything been settled? Yes to all of that—but even just that brief look shattered the comfort of Paradise for a moment.

Now his perspective has completely shifted. His new joy isn't simply that he's *in* Paradise. His new joy is that he's *not in Hell*. That becomes his overwhelming source of gratitude.

The first thing he says: "أَفَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ—Are we really not going to be dying anymore? Really?"

He already knew that. But he's saying it because he's in shock at how great it is to not be in Hell.

translation: Except for our first death, and we will not be punished
commentary:
إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ

"Except the first death we already tasted—that's over, right? It's not going to happen again? And we're never going to be tortured again, right? We're never going to be punished?"

Who talks about torture when they're in Paradise? But this person was so overwhelmed with gratitude for being in Paradise—not because of the drinks, not because of the beautiful company, not because of the spouse who's like a hidden treasure, not because of the lofty couches or the endless fruits—he's happy because he's *not in Hell*.

translation: Indeed, this is the great attainment
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

"No doubt, this is the ultimate success." For this person, الفوز العظيم—the great victory, the supreme achievement—is not being tortured. Because he saw what it's like. He witnessed what his friend is experiencing, and that glimpse transformed his entire understanding of Paradise.

This is the ultimate success.

translation: For the like of this let the workers [on earth] work
commentary:
لِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا فَلْيَعْمَلِ ٱلْعَـٰمِلُونَ

For the likes of this, let those who work put themselves to work. This statement comes after the detailed description of Paradise and its blessings. Allah ﷻ is telling us that anyone who is going to exert themselves, anyone who is going to put in effort and labor in this life, better direct that work toward achieving this ultimate reward. The workers, those who strive and toil, should set their sights on securing that company in Paradise, on being at the top of the cliff where the blessed reside, not at the bottom where the people of the Fire dwell.

This ayah serves as a powerful call to action. It's not just a gentle suggestion—it's an urgent wake-up call. Get your act together now, not later. Direct your efforts, your energy, your ambitions toward what truly matters.

translation: Is Paradise a better accommodation or the tree of zaqqum
commentary:
أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُّزُلًا أَمْ شَجَرَةُ ٱلزَّقُّومِ

Now Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question that forces reflection: Have you not concluded? Is that better as نُزُل—as introductory hospitality? Or do you think the Tree of Zaqqum is better as the introductory punishment of Hellfire?

Remember that nuzul refers to the initial hospitality offered to guests, the first meal or refreshment they receive. In Paradise, this was described beautifully in the preceding ayahs. But here, Allah ﷻ contrasts it with the "hospitality" of Hellfire—the Tree of Zaqqum. This tree resembles a cactus that grows in the desert, but far worse. It's covered in thorns, with green and orange outgrowths sprouting from the top, each bearing their own thorns. Even if you somehow removed all the thorns and attempted to eat it, the fruit itself is nearly poisonous, intensely bitter and sour.

This is the early snack. This is just getting started in the punishment of Hell.

translation: Indeed, We have made it a torment for the wrongdoers
commentary:
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهَا فِتْنَةً لِّلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

We have made it as a trial, as a test for the wrongdoers. The word فِتْنَة here carries multiple layers of meaning. In the Hereafter, it serves as a test for the wrongdoers themselves—how much can they endure? Each level of punishment tests their capacity before they're taken to deeper levels of torment.

But there's another dimension to this fitnah that occurred in this world. Whenever the Tree of Zaqqum was mentioned in the Quran, the disbelievers would mock it. They would say, "What kind of tree is this? You yourself said in the Quran that fire is made from wood, and fire consumes trees. How can a tree survive in fire, let alone grow in it? This doesn't make sense!" They used this as an excuse to reject the message entirely.

So the Tree of Zaqqum became a test of their faith in the unseen. Would they accept what Allah ﷻ revealed even when it challenged their limited understanding? Or would they reject the entire message based on their inability to comprehend one element? They chose mockery over submission. And now, Allah ﷻ tells them that the very tree they made fun of is growing for them. When they open their mouths to laugh and mock, they'll keep their mouths open as the Zaqqum is stuffed inside.

translation: Indeed, it is a tree issuing from the bottom of the Hellfire
commentary:
إِنَّهَا شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِىٓ أَصْلِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

It is a tree that comes out from the root of Hellfire, from the أَصْل—the origin, the very depth and foundation of Hell itself. This tree extends from the deepest pit of Jahannam all the way to the top, where the entrance to Hellfire is located. Its stem and major branches stretch through every level of punishment, making it accessible to the inhabitants throughout Hell's depths.

translation: Its emerging fruit as if it was heads of the devils
commentary:
طَلْعُهَا كَأَنَّهُۥ رُءُوسُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ

Its emerging fruit is as though it were the heads of devils. The word طَلْع refers to the fruit or buds that emerge from a tree. But instead of normal fruit, this vicious, nasty-looking growth appears at the tips of the branches. Rather than bearing nourishing produce, the tree bears what looks like heads of devils—grotesque, spiked, horrifying to look at. These are the "fruits" that the inhabitants of Hell are forced to eat.

The comparison to devils' heads captures both the ugliness and the terror of this punishment. Even the appearance of what they must consume is designed to horrify and torment them.

translation: And indeed, they will eat from it and fill with it their bellies
commentary:
فَإِنَّهُمْ لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِنْهَا فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ

Then they, no doubt about it, are going to be eating from it. فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ—then their stomachs are going to be filled with it.

Notice the contrast here with what we learn about Paradise. In Jannah, you drink and eat, but you're never uncomfortably full. You can keep taking more without ever feeling stuffed or sick. But in Hell, the opposite occurs—the stomachs become filled, bloated, painfully stuffed with this torturous food.

translation: Then indeed, they will have after it a mixture of scalding water
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنَّ لَهُمْ عَلَيْهَا لَشَوْبًا مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ

Then on top of that, they're going to have شَوْب—a mixture, a cocktail. The word shawb refers to something mixed together. It's actually one of the words used for honey in Arabic (ash-shuwbah) because Arabs would mix honey with everything—water, milk, and other substances.

But here, they're given a drink that has been mixed with various things, and one of those components is حَمِيم—scalding, purulent fluid; essentially pus. After eating the thorny, bitter fruit of Zaqqum, with its spikes tearing at their insides, they cry out, "Drink, drink, please!" The thorns are ripping their throats and stomachs apart, and they desperately need something to drink. But what they're given to drink is this horrific mixture that only adds to their torment.

translation: Then indeed, their return will be to the Hellfire
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنَّ مَرْجِعَهُمْ لَإِلَى ٱلْجَحِيمِ

Then their return is definitely to Jaheem. This reveals something important: that was just the introductory snack. That was just getting warmed up. After the Tree of Zaqqum and the drink of hamīm, they are taken to Jaheem—the deeper, more intense levels of Hell.

Remember earlier in the surah when Allah ﷻ described someone who saw a person in the middle of Hell? That person wasn't eating from the Tree of Zaqqum. He wasn't drinking that mixture. He was already in the middle, already past that initial stage. The Tree of Zaqqum is just the lobby of Hellfire. It's just the starting point. What comes after, what lies deeper inside, is beyond description—so terrible that it cannot even be put into words. After the lobby comes the real punishment.

translation: Indeed they found their fathers astray
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ أَلْفَوْا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ ضَآلِّينَ

They found their fathers lost. The word أَلْفَوْا means they discovered, they encountered. These people saw clearly that their fathers were misguided, that they were following a baseless religion built on false traditions and empty rituals.

translation: So they hastened [to follow] in their footsteps
commentary:
فَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ يُهْرَعُونَ

Yet even though they found their fathers lost and knew their religion was baseless, they would يُهْرَعُونَ—leap, rush, race at the opportunity to commit themselves to the traditions and traces left by their ancestors. The word yuhra'ūn suggests rushing forward with enthusiasm and urgency.

They saw clearly that what their forefathers practiced was wrong, yet they eagerly followed in their footsteps anyway. They embraced the remnants and traditions their ancestors left behind, refusing to break free from that cycle of misguidance.

This connects to what we learned earlier in the surah about آثَار—the remnants, the legacy that people leave behind. Ancestors who leave bad traditions behind them, and then generation after generation blindly follows those traditions—both the ancestors and all the generations that follow them will pay the price. Each generation that perpetuates the misguidance shares in the burden.

I think of people I know who converted away from Islam—to Christianity, to become Jehovah's Witnesses—for immigration status, for worldly benefit. Religion wasn't a big deal in their lives to begin with, so they figured, "Why not? It can bring me something good in this life." The church sponsored their papers, got them a top-notch lawyer, helped them get asylum or citizenship. And now they're raising entire families as Christians. That original person who made that choice will answer not only for himself, but for every single one of those children, and their children, and all the generations that follow. I feel immense sadness for people like that—for what they've set in motion.

translation: And there had already strayed before them most of the former peoples
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

And most of those who came in history before them were lost as well. This wasn't a new phenomenon. Throughout history, the majority of earlier nations also went astray, following their forefathers blindly into misguidance.

translation: And We had already sent among them warners
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِم مُّنذِرِينَ

And We had sent warners in their midst. Allah ﷻ didn't leave them without guidance. He sent messengers among them, warners who came to pull them out of that cycle of inherited falsehood.

translation: Then look how was the end of those who were warned
commentary:
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ

Then take a look yourself—what became of those who were warned? This is an invitation to the Prophet ﷺ and to us: observe the ruins of past civilizations, study their histories. See what happened to those nations who received warners but rejected them.

translation: But not the chosen servants of Allah
commentary:
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

Except the slaves of Allah, the ones who have been granted divine sincerity. The word مُخْلَصِين here is particularly significant—these are the ones whom Allah ﷻ Himself has purified, the ones granted sincerity directly from Allah. They were saved from the destruction that befell those nations.

translation: And Noah had certainly called Us, and [We are] the best of responders
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ نَادَىٰنَا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ ٱلْمُجِيبُونَ

And Nuh (AS) had made a call to Us. فَلَنِعْمَ ٱلْمُجِيبُونَ—then We are the best of those who respond.

There's a beautiful irony in this phrase. When Allah ﷻ says He responded immediately, we might think, "But wait—Nuh (AS) called to his people for 950 years! How is that an immediate response?" From our limited perspective, it seems like Allah ﷻ took His time. But Allah ﷻ calls it an immediate response because He exists beyond time. What feels like centuries to us is not delayed from His perspective.

Allah ﷻ knows when to send the response, and He is the best of those who respond. The response He sends is not necessarily the answer you want—it's the answer you need. What you want might be immature, impulsive, shortsighted. What you need, Allah ﷻ knows better. So He responds immediately with what is best for you, whether you see it that way or not, whether you recognize it as an answer or not.

translation: And We saved him and his family from the great affliction
commentary:
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

And We rescued him and his family from the great distress. The word كَرْبrefers to a disturbance that brings about distress, sadness, fear and anxiety. After all those centuries of calling his people, being rejected, mocked and threatened, after the buildup to the flood and the catastrophic event itself, Allah ﷻ rescued Nuh (AS) and his believing family from that immense trial.

translation: And We made his descendants those remaining [on the earth]
commentary:
وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥ هُمُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ

And We made his offspring the only ones to remain. Even though other people boarded the ship with Nuh (AS)—other believers who survived the flood—what we learn here is that their generations didn't continue. The only lineage that survived and continued through history was that of the believing sons of Nuh (AS).

We know about one son who didn't come on board, who chose to climb a mountain instead and drowned. But Nuh (AS) had other sons who believed and were saved. It's possible that after the flood, Nuh (AS) married again and started another family. Humanity essentially began anew through his lineage.

We know of three sons specifically: Sam, Ham and Yafith. All of humanity descends from their lineage. Ibrahim (AS) comes from the line of Sam. Every prophet mentioned in the Quran after Nuh (AS) traces their lineage back to these sons.

translation: And left for him [favorable mention] among later generations
commentary:
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

And We left for him among the later generations. Allah ﷻ preserved mention of Nuh (AS), honor for him, commitment to his path among all the nations that came after him.

translation: Peace upon Noah among the worlds
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Peace be upon Nuh among all the nations. In the previous surah, we learned that سَلَام from Allah ﷻ is something special, reserved for particular individuals. It's not given to everyone—it's an honor bestowed upon the prophets. Here, Allah ﷻ Himself sends salam upon Nuh (AS) throughout all the worlds, across all nations and all times.

translation: Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

Indeed, this is how We compensate those who excel. The word مُحْسِنِين comes from إحْسَان—excellence, perfection in worship, doing something beautifully. This is Allah's promise: those who excel in their devotion, who perfect their worship and their character, will be rewarded in this magnificent way.

translation: Indeed, he was of Our believing servants
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

No doubt he is from Our believing slaves. After all the honors mentioned, Allah ﷻ concludes by identifying Nuh (AS) by his most essential quality: he was a believing slave of Allah.

translation: Then We drowned the disbelievers
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ

Then, after We rescued him, We drowned all the others who were left behind. The contrast is stark and final.

translation: And indeed, among his kind was Abraham
commentary:
وَإِنَّ مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ لَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ

And from his same faction, from his same thinking, from his same ideology, from his same group—Ibrahim (AS). The word شِيعَة means a group united by common ideology and purpose. Ibrahim (AS) belonged to the same spiritual faction as Nuh (AS), even though they were separated by many generations.

From the lineage of Sam, one of Nuh's sons, through many generations, Ibrahim (AS) was eventually born in the ancient city of Ur in Iraq. So there's both a spiritual connection—they shared the same mission, the same message—and a physical lineage connecting Ibrahim (AS) back to Nuh (AS).

translation: When he came to his Lord with a sound heart
commentary:
إِذْ جَآءَ رَبَّهُۥ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ

When he came to his Master with a قَلْب سَلِيم (qalb salīm)—a clean, sound, healthy heart.

This is described even before Ibrahim (AS) became a messenger. Before receiving prophethood, before any revelation came to him, he came before Allah ﷻ with a sound heart. There's something profound here about the prerequisite for guidance.

There are two dimensions I want you to understand. There's a sound mind and a sound heart—عَقْل سَلِيم and قَلْب سَلِيم. Your mind will reach the right conclusions if the heart is sound. But if the heart isn't sound, you could be a genius—it won't matter. You won't arrive at guidance. You have to come to Allah ﷻ with a sound, healthy, good heart.

What does that sound heart mean? It refers to what Allah ﷻ described in Surah Ar-Rum: فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ—the natural disposition, the original mold that Allah fashioned. It's the good nature Allah ﷻ created within you and poured into you when you were about 120 days in your mother's womb. That pure nature, that innate goodness—if you can maintain it, if you can preserve that inside of you, then revelation will make sense to you. The Quran will resonate. You'll arrive at the right conclusions.

I'm reminded of a story about a man from the generation connected to the Prophet ﷺ. He used to hold the cover of the Kaaba and cry, saying, "I don't know how to worship You, but I want to. Please teach me how." He had a good heart. He just didn't know the way yet. That's the qalb salīm.

translation: [And] when he said to his father and his people, "What do you worship
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَاذَا تَعْبُدُونَ

When he said to his father and his people: What are you worshipping? This question is direct, confrontational. There's no elaborate argument about the sun and moon here, as we saw with other prophets reasoning with their people. Ibrahim (AS) has moved past that stage. His people aren't listening to gradual reasoning, so he becomes explicit.

translation: Is it falsehood [as] gods other than Allah you desire
commentary:
أَئِفْكًا ءَالِهَةً دُونَ ٱللَّهِ تُرِيدُونَ

These vicious lies—gods other than Allah—is this what you want? أَئِفْك refers to a complete fabrication, a vicious lie turned upside down from the truth.

translation: Then what is your thought about the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَمَا ظَنُّكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

So what are your thoughts about the Master of all the worlds? Why don't you think about that? Why don't you consider that there's a real Master, a true Lord over all creation?

We've seen different prophets use different forms of reasoning with their people. Some pointed gradually toward the sky, toward natural phenomena, letting people slowly reach conclusions. But now Ibrahim (AS) is past that point. They're not listening to subtle guidance, so he directly challenges them: What do you think about the actual Master of the universe?

translation: And he cast a look at the stars
commentary:
فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِى ٱلنُّجُومِ

Then he stared at the stars. He looked intently at the stars, and you can sense the disgust in that gaze. His people used to have a religious festival where they would worship various objects, including the stars. They would leave the city to go celebrate this massive festival dedicated to their false gods.

Ibrahim (AS) looked at those same stars—the stars they worshipped, the stars that should actually guide people to Allah ﷻ—and he felt sick.

translation: And said, "Indeed, I am [about to be] ill
commentary:
فَقَالَ إِنِّى سَقِيمٌ

He said: I am sick. And he wasn't lying. He was sick—sick of their religion, sick to his stomach at what they were doing. The word سَقِيم means genuinely ill, and Ibrahim (AS) was disgusted to the point of feeling physically sick at their worship.

So when his father and others said, "Let's go, it's the big festival, everyone's going," he looked at the stars they worshipped and said, "I'm not coming with you. I'm sick."

translation: So they turned away from him, departing
commentary:
فَتَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ

Then they turned away from him, putting their backs to him. They left him behind and went to their festival, leaving the village essentially empty. Ibrahim (AS) was the only one who stayed back.

translation: Then he turned to their gods and said, "Do you not eat
commentary:
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِهِمْ فَقَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ

So he turned toward their gods and said: Are you not eating? The people had placed food, flowers, milk and various offerings in front of these idols before leaving for the festival. Ibrahim (AS) approached these statues, stared at the food laid before them, and mockingly asked, "You're not going to eat?"

He's expressing his frustration face-to-face with these lifeless objects. You can sense that he's a young man at this point, full of conviction and courage.

translation: What is [wrong] with you that you do not speak
commentary:
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنطِقُونَ

What's wrong with you? You don't talk back? You can't say anything? He's directly confronting the absurdity of worshipping something that can't speak, can't respond, can't do anything.

translation: And he turned upon them a blow with [his] right hand
commentary:
فَرَاغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْبًۢا بِٱلْيَمِينِ

Then he turned on them, striking with his right hand. At first, he smacked one of them with his right hand. The word يَمِين emphasizes the right hand, suggesting strength and determination.

After that first strike, when the idol fell to the ground, he went to town on it. The Quran alludes to what happened next without spelling out every detail—we know from other passages that he destroyed the idols, leaving only the largest one standing with the axe in its hand, as if to suggest that idol had destroyed the others.

translation: Then the people came toward him, hastening
commentary:
فَأَقْبَلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ

Then they came at him, rushing forward, having lost their minds. When the people returned from their festival and discovered what had happened, they came at Ibrahim (AS) in a frenzy, completely out of control. "What have you done? What have you done?"

translation: He said, "Do you worship that which you [yourselves] carve
commentary:
قَالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مَا تَنْحِتُونَ

He said: Do you worship what you yourselves have carved? This is the heart of his argument laid bare. You're worshipping something that you made with your own hands. You carved it, you shaped it, you painted it—and then you bow down to it?

translation: While Allah created you and that which you do
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

And Allah created you and everything that you do. This is a profound statement with layers of meaning. On the surface level, it means: Allah ﷻ created you, and He also created the idols you make. You carved them, yes, but Allah ﷻ created the materials, created your hands, created your ability to carve. So ultimately, Allah ﷻ created even the idols you worship.

But some scholars, particularly those with philosophical inclinations, have taken this statement to a deeper level. They've understood it to mean that Allah ﷻ created us and He also created our actions. They separated our actions from their results. You can do whatever you want—you can exercise, you can eat right, you can work hard—but the results, the outcomes of those actions, aren't ultimately in your control. Those results are Allah's creation.

The action may be yours, but the results belong to Allah ﷻ. Your health, despite all your efforts, is in His hands. Your success, despite all your planning, is His creation. This understanding humbles us and reminds us that we're not in complete control.

translation: They said, "Construct for him a furnace and throw him into the burning fire
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ لَهُۥ بُنْيَـٰنًا فَأَلْقُوهُ فِى ٱلْجَحِيمِ

They said: Build a building for him, then throw him into the blazing fire! Their response reveals their desperation. Rather than engage with his arguments, they resort to violence.

But notice the method: they don't just build a regular fire. They say build a بُنْيَان—a massive structure, a tower. You build this enormous tower, this construction that stands on its own, then you set the entire structure ablaze. You keep throwing fuel on it, building the fire higher and higher until it becomes this towering inferno.

Then, once the fire is roaring, you throw him into الْجَحِيم—the loud, roaring flame. The fire itself would sound like thunder, like a roar. That's when you cast him in.

translation: And they intended for him a plan, but We made them the most debased
commentary:
فَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ

Then they wanted to plan something against him, to execute a scheme against him. People were starting to be impressed by Ibrahim (AS), starting to question their own beliefs. His people wanted to terrify this young man, to make an example of him. They thought if they grabbed him and prepared to throw him into this massive fire, he would come to his senses. They expected him to break down: "Sorry, sorry, sorry! I promise I'll never do anything like that again!"
