But he didn't turn back. He didn't recant. فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَسْفَلِينَ—so We made them the lowest. Allah ﷻ keeps it ambiguous for us, not spelling out exactly what He did to them. It means they were humiliated, brought low. It could also mean they were destroyed, that they perished. We don't know the exact details, but we know Allah ﷻ lowered them, defeated them completely.

What we do know for certain is that Ibrahim (AS) escaped unharmed.

translation: And [then] he said, "Indeed, I will go to [where I am ordered by] my Lord; He will guide me
commentary:
وَقَالَ إِنِّى ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ

And he said: I am going to my Master; He will guide me. Look at the beautiful faith of Ibrahim (AS) in this statement. He's leaving Iraq, heading toward Palestine. This wasn't a simple journey. Between Iraq and Palestine lies the Syrian desert—a vast, deadly expanse you cannot cross directly because you won't survive. Travelers had to go north along the Euphrates River (the Furat), following its course to avoid perishing in the desert. It was a prolonged, difficult journey.

And Ibrahim (AS) doesn't say, "I'm going to Palestine." He doesn't name the destination. He says, "I'm going to my Master"—as though the destination isn't a place, but Allah ﷻ Himself. "I'm going to Allah ﷻ, and He will guide me."

Eventually, he reaches Palestine. Later, from there, the journey continues to Makkah, where he's commanded to make hijrah, to leave his wife Hajar and infant son Ismail (AS) in that barren valley. The story continues from there—the establishment of the Kaaba, the sacrifice, all the trials that follow.

translation: My Lord, grant me [a child] from among the righteous
commentary:
رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

My Master, grant me from among the righteous. This dua can be understood in multiple ways. It could mean: grant me righteous children. Or it could mean: grant me righteous company, righteous companions in this lonely journey.

And Allah ﷻ grants him both—righteous children and righteous companionship through them.

translation: So We gave him good tidings of a forbearing boy
commentary:
فَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ حَلِيمٍ

Then We gave him glad tidings of a حَلِيم boy—a forbearing, loving, compassionate, patient and tolerant child. The word halīm is extraordinary. It describes someone who doesn't get overwhelmed, who doesn't overreact. Someone who can absorb difficulties and deal with them in a calm, measured way. It's someone who has emotional maturity and stability.

Halīm is such a rare quality that in the entire Quran, other than when it's used to describe Allah ﷻ Himself, it's only used for two individuals: it's used for Ibrahim (AS), and it's used for Ismail (AS). That's it. Those are the only two people in all of the Quran described with this characteristic.

This tells us something profound about the boy being promised here—this is Ismail (AS), and he will carry his father's most beautiful quality.

translation: And when he reached with him [the age of] exertion, he said, "O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think." He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast
commentary:
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ ٱلسَّعْىَ قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ فِى ٱلْمَنَامِ أَنِّىٓ أَذْبَحُكَ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ ٱفْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

When the son reached an age where he could work alongside his father, Ibrahim (AS) shared the vision he had been given. The words of this ayah reveal the profound gentleness in how he approached this unimaginable test: "O my son, I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you, so tell me what you think."

The response of the young boy demonstrates a maturity and submission that surpasses understanding. He says, "O my father, do what you are commanded." Notice the wisdom in his words—he doesn't say "do what you want" or "do what you think is best." He recognizes this as a divine command, not his father's personal desire. This is a young person telling his father to proceed with slaughtering him, yet he does so with complete clarity about the source of the command.

Then comes a phrase that many of us use casually today: إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ. But this isn't the "insha'Allah" we often use nowadays that really means "probably not." When we ask someone, "Are you coming?" and they say "insha'Allah," we understand they likely won't show up. But the son's use of this phrase carries profound theological depth. He says, "You will find me, if Allah ﷻ wills, among the patient ones."

He's not expressing doubt about his own resolve. Rather, he's acknowledging a fundamental truth: his ability to be patient depends entirely on the will of Allah ﷻ. He's saying, "My patience is not something I possess independently. My ability to endure this test comes only from Allah ﷻ, so I leave that matter to Him." This is submission at its highest level—recognizing that even our strength to obey comes from the One we're obeying.

translation: And when they had both submitted and he put him down upon his forehead
commentary:
فَلَمَّآ أَسْلَمَا وَتَلَّهُۥ لِلْجَبِينِ

The ayah uses the word أَسْلَمَا—"when both of them submitted." Both had to surrender themselves completely. For the father, this meant preparing to sacrifice the joy and coolness of his eyes, his young son running around. For the son, this meant surrendering his young life. Neither submission was easy, yet both were complete.

The phrase وَتَلَّهُۥ لِلْجَبِينِ describes the physical act: he laid him down on the side of his forehead. The word تَلَّ means to make someone lie on their face. This is significant—Ibrahim (AS) didn't lay him down flat on his back. He positioned him on the side of his face, the way an animal is positioned for slaughter. The reality of what was about to happen was fully present in that moment.

translation: We called to him, "O Abraham
commentary:
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ أَن يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ

According to some narrations, Ibrahim (AS) attempted to cut with the knife—he tried and tried, but the blade wouldn't penetrate. Just as Allah ﷻ commanded the fire not to burn Ibrahim (AS), He commanded the knife not to cut. Imagine the emotional weight of that moment—attempting again and again while his son lay there. How painful must that have been?

Then the call came: "O Ibrahim!" We called out to him, and relief arrived.

translation: You have fulfilled the vision." Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ ٱلرُّءْيَآ ۚ إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

"You have fulfilled the vision." The test was complete. He had confirmed the truth of what he saw in his dream through his actions. The command comes: you can stop now. You don't have to continue. The test is over.

Then Allah ﷻ establishes a principle: إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ—"Indeed, this is how We compensate those who excel." This is how We reward the people of ihsan, those who worship Allah ﷻ as though they see Him. The reward for excellence in submission is divine intervention and eternal honor.

translation: Indeed, this was the clear trial
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْبَلَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْمُبِينُ

"Indeed, that was truly the clear trial." The word بَلَاء in Arabic refers to a severe test, a difficult and traumatic experience. Allah ﷻ Himself describes this as الْبَلَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْمُبِينُ—the clear, unmistakable, heavy test. This is a trial like no other, a test that stands alone in its difficulty and its demand for complete submission.

translation: And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice
commentary:
وَفَدَيْنَـٰهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ

"And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice." The word فَدَى means to ransom or exchange. Allah ﷻ provided an animal as a substitute and said, "Sacrifice this instead."

This is where we learn the origins of Eid al-Adha. It begins here—with a man who saw a dream commanding him to sacrifice his own child, and with a child who submitted completely before Allah ﷻ, even if it meant losing his own life. We are learning from the prophets the ultimate example of what it means to submit before Allah ﷻ.

When I reflect on this passage, ayah 66 of Surah An-Nisa comes to mind: "And if We had commanded them, 'Kill yourselves' or 'Leave your homes,' they would not have done it, except for a few of them." Hypothetically, if Allah ﷻ revealed a command to kill yourself or kill your own family or abandon your homes—both of which were done by Ibrahim (AS)—most people wouldn't do it. They would say, "This doesn't make any sense. How could I slaughter my own child? What kind of God would make me do that? I don't want this religion."

But Ibrahim (AS) came to his Lord with a sound heart—a قَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ. His heart had completely surrendered to Allah ﷻ. His intellect submitted to his heart, because if you leave everything to the intellect alone, it will always ask "why?" Why would I jump into a fire? Why would I put a knife to my son's neck? These are matters of the heart, which is why the heart had to be mentioned in Ibrahim's story. Allah ﷻ says most people wouldn't have done it, but had they done it, it would have been better for them.

When we sacrifice an animal on Eid al-Adha—which will soon be here—what are we supposed to remember? As we slaughter the animal and enjoy its meat, we're supposed to recall a man who was willing to do anything Allah ﷻ asked, even the last thing anyone would be willing to do in their life. He was willing to do it because Allah ﷻ commanded it.

As you and I are performing that sacrifice, we should be asking ourselves: What is Allah ﷻ asking me to do that I'm not willing to do yet? What am I not comfortable with? What am I easing into, waiting for the right time? That hesitation isn't a matter of intellect—it's a matter of the heart. Something is missing, something that isn't making us follow the legacy of Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And We left for him [favorable mention] among later generations
commentary:
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

"And We left for him among later generations." The sacrifice tradition and this legacy survived through all the generations to come, all the way to the arrival of Muhammad ﷺ.

There's an important historical point here. Some scholars in Islamic history, relying on Israeli narrations, believed the son to be sacrificed was Ishaq (AS). A few contemporary scholars hold this view as well. However, based on what I've studied, I must share my conviction: there is overwhelming evidence that it was Ismail (AS).

I recently discovered a book by Hameeduddin Farahi, an Indian scholar who wrote in Arabic about 100 years ago. The book is titled "The Correct Opinion About Who Was Slaughtered," and it examines Christian, Jewish and Muslim accounts along with historical data. It has been translated into Urdu and reportedly into Arabic as well. One of our students has pledged to translate it into English, which will make this research more widely available.

Consider this: if the sacrifice tradition belonged to the Jewish people through Ishaq (AS), why were the pagan Arabs—who knew nothing of Jewish scripture—slaughtering animals in the name of Ismail and Ibrahim for thousands of years, even before the coming of the Prophet ﷺ? Why was Hajj a tradition for them? The Ka'bah was built by Ibrahim and Ismail (AS), not Ishaq (AS).

Our scholars discovered that some Jewish scholars, desperate to maintain that Isaac was the honored son—because they couldn't accept that Ishmael, ancestor of the gentiles, would be honored—renamed a valley in Jerusalem "Baca," attempting to appropriate the honor of the valley of Makkah, which the Quran calls Bakkah. The lengths some will go to preserve their narrative are remarkable.

translation: Peace upon Abraham
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ

"Peace be upon Ibrahim." Allah ﷻ Himself extends salutations upon him. After everything we've learned about this prophet—his willingness to sacrifice everything, his complete submission—how can anyone say anything dishonorable about him? How can anyone accuse him of an illegitimate relationship or claim that his child wasn't legitimate, all to hold onto claims of supremacy? It's incredible. Allah ﷻ honors him with His salutations.

translation: Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

The principle is repeated: "Thus do We reward those who excel." This is the compensation for ihsan.

translation: Indeed, he was of Our believing servants
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

"Indeed, he was one of Our believing servants." Allah ﷻ declares Ibrahim's status among His truly faithful slaves.

translation: And We gave him good tidings of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous
commentary:
وَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِإِسْحَـٰقَ نَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

"And We gave him good news of Ishaq, a prophet from among the righteous." This ayah provides crucial chronological evidence. Allah ﷻ congratulated Ibrahim (AS) with the news of Ishaq (AS) after the events of the sacrifice had already been mentioned. This tells us definitively who the son to be sacrificed was, because if Ishaq is being given as good news later, Ishaq could never have been the only son. When the Bible says "sacrifice your only son," that can only apply to the older son, which is Ismail (AS).

The way some attempt to reconcile this is to claim that Hajar's relationship was illegitimate, that Ismail wasn't a legitimate child. But you would dishonor Ibrahim (AS) by making such a claim? You're willing to disrespect this noble prophet and even dishonor Hajar (AS) just to maintain your position? And after Allah ﷻ has sent salutations upon Ibrahim (AS), how can such accusations stand?

Why would Allah ﷻ congratulate Ibrahim (AS) with a child described as حَلِيم—forbearing and wise—if there was anything inappropriate about the circumstances? The logic collapses under examination.

translation: And We blessed him and Isaac. But among their descendants is the doer of good and the clearly unjust to himself
commentary:
وَبَـٰرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلَىٰٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا مُحْسِنٌ وَظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ مُبِينٌ

"And We blessed him and Ishaq. But among their descendants are those who excel and those who clearly wrong themselves." Allah ﷻ showered blessings upon both Ibrahim and Ishaq (AS). From their offspring came many great people, but there were also deeply disappointing ones—those who clearly wronged themselves.

Among the immediate descendants of Ishaq (AS), we see this contrast. You have Ya'qub (AS), but then you also have the brothers of Yusuf (AS)—his own grandchildren who plotted against their brother. Eventually, their lineage continues to the followers of Musa (AS), but among those too, you find both the excellent and the rebellious. This is the reality of human generations: muhsin (those who excel) and dhalim li nafsihi mubeen (those who clearly wrong themselves).

## Ayahs 114-116

وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ - وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُمَا وَقَوْمَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ - وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ

Speaking of the descendants of Ishaq (AS) who were excellent, Allah ﷻ highlights two great heroes: "And We certainly bestowed favor upon Musa and Harun."

"We rescued them and their nation from the great distress." Notice that the word كَرْب—distress or anguish—is used both for the people of Nuh (AS) and for the people of Musa (AS). In both cases, the destruction was connected to water: the flood for Nuh's people, and the sea for Fir'awn's army.

"Then We aided them, and they were the ones who dominated." Allah ﷻ gave them victory, and they became the dominant ones. This brief mention sets the stage for the detailed stories of Musa and Harun (AS) that will follow in the surah.

translation: And We did certainly confer favor upon Moses and Aaron
And We saved them and their people from the great affliction
And We supported them so it was they who overcame
And We gave them the explicit Scripture
commentary:
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلْمُسْتَبِينَ

The conversation continues about Musa and Harun (AS) and the compliments Allah ﷻ bestows upon them. Allah ﷻ says, "We gave both of them the book al-mustabeen" - that which is clear in and of itself. This word المُسْتَبِينَ is similar to the term مُبِين, but it carries a unique distinction as it appears only once in the Quran in this exact form. Al-mustabeen refers to that which has reached the highest level of clarity, the pinnacle of being able to be understood clearly.

Allah ﷻ also describes the book elsewhere as صِرَاط- the straight path. The description here emphasizes that the Torah given to Musa and Harun (AS) was absolutely, incredibly clear. This clarity is mentioned because there should be no confusion about who was to be sacrificed in the story of Ibrahim (AS), nor about the true character of Ibrahim (AS), since what was given to Musa and Harun (AS) was very clear about these teachings.

translation: And We guided them on the straight path
commentary:
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ

Allah ﷻ says He guided both Musa and Harun (AS) to the straight path - الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ.

translation: And We left for them [favorable mention] among later generations
commentary:
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِمَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

And We left upon them - meaning We left their legacies - among the people that would come until the very end, in all the generations to follow.

translation: Peace upon Moses and Aaron
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ

Salutations and peace upon Musa and Harun (AS).

translation: Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

No doubt, that is how We compensate those who excel - المُحْسِنِينَ, those who do beautiful, excellent deeds.

translation: Indeed, they were of Our believing servants
commentary:
إِنَّهُمَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

They were truly, both of them, from Our believing slaves.

translation: And indeed, Elias was from among the messengers
commentary:
وَإِنَّ إِلْيَاسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

Ilyas (Elias) (AS) was no doubt among those who were sent as messengers. He was from the lineage of Harun (AS) and lived in the city of Ba'labakk. Ba'labakk is a famous city in what is now Lebanon and Jordan. The name is actually two words: "Bakk" is an ancient word for city or a place of high traffic - this root is related to Makkah. "Ba'al" is the name of a false god they used to worship. So Ba'labakk literally means "the city that belongs to Ba'al." This was an old, pre-Arab name.

translation: When he said to his people, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

When he said to his nation: "Why do you not want to protect yourselves? Why do you not take any caution?"

translation: Do you call upon Ba'l and leave the best of creators
commentary:
أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلًا وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ

"Are you calling on Ba'al, the false god? And you are abandoning the best of all creators?"

translation: Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers
commentary:
ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

"Allah ﷻ, your Master and the Master of your earliest ancestors?"

translation: And they denied him, so indeed, they will be brought [for punishment]
commentary:
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ

Then they lied against him, or they called him a liar. But they will be from those who are made to stand in line for judgment. We have seen this scene already in this surah.

translation: Except the chosen servants of Allah
commentary:
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

The exception will be the slaves of Allah ﷻ who are granted sincerity - المُخْلَصِينَ.

translation: And We left for him [favorable mention] among later generations
commentary:
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

And We left some of his followers, some committed to his way, also in those final ones to come.

translation: Peace upon Elias
commentary:
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِلْ يَاسِينَ

May peace be upon Il Yaseen - Ilyas and his followers. The word إِلْيَاسِينَ as opposed to إِلْيَاسَ (Ilyas) - the addition of the ـِينَ at the end is understood by some scholars to mean "those who are like Ilyas," meaning those who follow the legacy of Ilyas (AS). It's similar to how in English we might say "Abrahamic" to refer to followers of Ibrahim (AS), or "Mosaic" to refer to the tradition of Musa (AS). Europeans used to call Muslims "Mohammedans." So Il Yaseen may mean Ilyas and those who followed with him.

translation: Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

No doubt, that is how We compensate those who excel.

translation: Indeed, he was of Our believing servants
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

No doubt he was among Our believing slaves.

translation: And indeed, Lot was among the messengers
commentary:
وَإِنَّ لُوطًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

And no doubt, Lut (Lot) (AS) was among those who were sent.

translation: [So mention] when We saved him and his family, all
commentary:
إِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ

When We rescued him and his family, all together.

translation: Except his wife among those who remained [with the evildoers]
commentary:
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ

Except the old woman, the wretched old woman who stayed behind and did not catch up with them. الْغَابِرِينَ refers to those who stayed behind, who did not catch up.

translation: Then We destroyed the others
commentary:
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ

Then We crushed all the others, whoever remained.

translation: And indeed, you pass by them in the morning
commentary:
وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِم مُّصْبِحِينَ

And you are passing over them - the ruins of their city - in the morning.

translation: And at night. Then will you not use reason
commentary:
وَبِٱلَّيْلِ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

And by night. Why don't you apply your intellect? The city has been buried, yet you pass over it day and night without reflection.

translation: And indeed, Jonah was among the messengers
commentary:
وَإِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

No doubt, Yunus (Jonah) (AS) was truly from those who have been sent.

translation: [Mention] when he ran away to the laden ship
commentary:
إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ

When he fled to the loaded ship. The word أَبَقَ is one of several words for "to run away," but it's specifically used when a slave runs away from his master. This is the only context in which this word is used. The imagery here is powerful - when Yunus (AS) was frustrated with his people and left, Allah ﷻ is saying, "That was him running away from Me." He didn't realize it at the time. He left the city and boarded a filled-up ship - الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ, a ship that was completely loaded. Everyone was already on board and it was heading off, and he just jumped on as a straggler.

translation: And he drew lots and was among the losers
commentary:
فَسَاهَمَ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُدْحَضِينَ

Then he entered into a lottery, and he was from those who were cast off. The long story is captured in just one word - this is part of the eloquence of the Quran. سَاهَمَ means to cast lots, to throw lots. They were out at sea when a storm came, and there are different reasons given for why they decided to cast lots. Some say they believed one of them was cursed and not supposed to be there, so they needed to offer a sacrifice. Others say the boat was sinking and they had to let go of cargo. They threw all the cargo overboard, but it wasn't enough - some human beings had to be thrown into the ocean for the entire ship to survive. But who deserves to be thrown and who deserves to stay? It wasn't fair to just choose, so they made a raffle. Everyone put their name in and they drew names. Yunus (AS) joined that raffle - سَاهَمَ. 

فَكَانَ مِنَ الْمُدْحَضِينَ - then he was from those who were cast off. Anything that falls down is المُدْحَض - like an animal that's been slaughtered and just tips over. So off the plank of the ship - that's المُدْحَض as well. He was from those who were thrown off.

translation: Then the fish swallowed him, while he was blameworthy
commentary:
فَٱلْتَقَمَهُ ٱلْحُوتُ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ

Then the whale took him in one bite, in one swallow. الْتَقَمَ means to swallow something in one bite or to take it in one gulp. وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ - and he was filled with regret. Elsewhere in the Quran, Allah ﷻ talks about the regret Yunus (AS) was filled with and the tawbah he had to make.

But why would Allah ﷻ put somebody through that? There's a beautiful piece of Arabic poetry that says: "Reprimand is only most appropriate for those you love, and love gets better by means of correction. Its truth is proven." People you truly love, you will try to correct. That's what's happening here. If you truly love someone, you'll try to correct them. You don't just give up on them and say, "Forget them, I don't need to talk to them anymore." Real love won't let you abandon people. Sometimes your friend does something wrong and they're just no longer your friend. You don't call them back, don't talk to them anymore. Who cares? Life moves on. That wasn't real friendship. If it was real friendship, you'd make the effort to say, "Listen, what you said was wrong. Our friendship is too valuable for me not to say something." This correction, this التَّعْذِيب - this disciplining of the soul - cleanses relationships too.

translation: And had he not been of those who exalt Allah
commentary:
فَلَوْلَآ أَنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَبِّحِينَ

And had he not been from المُسَبِّحِينَ - those who continually declare Allah's perfection through tasbih.

translation: He would have remained inside its belly until the Day they are resurrected
commentary:
لَلَبِثَ فِى بَطْنِهِۦٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ

He would have remained in its belly until the day they are raised again. In other words, he would have died in there and his remains would have been preserved in that whale. Allah ﷻ would have preserved that whale just so Yunus (AS) could be resurrected from there on the Day of Judgment.

But it was tasbih that got him out of trouble. What is Allah ﷻ teaching us? When you have trouble and you're stuck in difficulty, what should you do? Do a lot of tasbih. Dua alone won't solve your problems, but tasbih will solve your problems. Allah ﷻ is giving us formulas to get out of trouble by means of this account.

translation: But We threw him onto the open shore while he was ill
commentary:
۞ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ

Then We had him thrown onto a dry patch of land - a small island or barren area - وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ, and he was sick. He suffered from malnutrition and his skin must have been damaged from being in water and that damp environment the whole time.

translation: And We caused to grow over him a gourd vine
commentary:
وَأَنۢبَتْنَا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِّن يَقْطِينٍ

And We sprouted for him a tree from يَقْطِينٍ. There are different opinions about what yaqteen is, and you can research various scholarly views on this plant. In other cultures, like in South Asia, they have a plant called "begin," and sometimes the letter is transliterated differently. It's the kind of plant that bugs don't come near - it's poisonous to insects but has a lot of healing power for human beings. So it was a special plant with medicinal properties. The point was that he could put it on himself to heal, and he wouldn't be attacked by creatures, bugs and other things, because he was extremely sick at the time.

translation: And We sent him to [his people of] a hundred thousand or more
commentary:
وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ مِا۟ئَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ

And We sent him to a hundred thousand أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ - or even more than that. So by the time he came back to his nation, a hundred thousand had become believers. He was now the leader of a huge nation.

translation: And they believed, so We gave them enjoyment [of life] for a time
commentary:
فَـَٔامَنُوا۟ فَمَتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

Then they all believed, فَمَتَّعْنَاهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ - so We allowed them to enjoy until a given time.

Allah ﷻ is showing us that there are two kinds of enjoyment. We've learned about the enjoyment of الْمُتْرَفِينَ - those living in luxury. They enjoy the luxuries of this life, and then they're destroyed because those luxuries delude them. But Allah ﷻ says when people truly believe, when a society truly becomes a Muslim society, then they enjoy this world also, in addition to enjoying the next world. It's not like they're in denial of this world. Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, "They would have eaten from above them and below them" - meaning life would have been good - "if they only established the teachings of the Quran and the previous scriptures." Allah ﷻ is telling us that life would be good if you just established the teachings of this Book. You can enjoy a good, worldly life as well, until a given time.

translation: So inquire of them, [O Muhammad], "Does your Lord have daughters while they have sons
commentary:
فَٱسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَلِرَبِّكَ ٱلْبَنَاتُ وَلَهُمُ ٱلْبَنُونَ

Now the surah returns to the original conversation about the false theology of the Quraysh. Allah ﷻ says: "Then ask them their verdict." We've been talking about messengers, and angels come to messengers. Earlier in this surah, Allah ﷻ began describing the angels as armies of Allah ﷻ. Yet these people say that the angels are daughters of Allah ﷻ. But when they themselves have daughters, what do they do? They bury them alive. The idea of having daughters is disgusting to them, yet very proudly in their theology, God has daughters.

"Does your Master have daughters while you have sons?"

There's one thing about Allah ﷻ having a son or daughter - whatever it is, it's shirk, it's wrong. That's not even the main point now. That's already obvious. The additional point here is this: you want to give to God what you wouldn't even take for yourself. You want to give God the leftovers of your life. God is not a priority to you - you are the priority. That's your ultimate shirk. The shirk between the lines here is that to God you give that which is inferior, and for yourself you take what's superior.

How does this practically manifest in the life of Muslims? What happens in some Muslim countries is that you have a few sons, and one of them is not doing very well in school. You realize he has no future, so you think, "We should make him a hafiz. At least he can memorize Quran. At least he could become a mullah or imam or something." Or a child is born with some kind of mental disorder or deficiency, and the thinking is, "He should study the religion." But the one who has potential? "He should go to medical school. He should become an engineer." 

So you want to give Allah ﷻ what you don't want for yourself? You want to give Him what you consider inferior? Maybe you haven't truly understood these ayahs before. Allah ﷻ isn't just saying you attribute daughters to Him and that's haram - attributing offspring is wrong, yes. But the deeper point is: you gave Him what you don't want for yourself.

Allah ﷻ says elsewhere in the Quran, describing people giving charity: "You wouldn't take something unless you have to squint your eyes to accept it" - your dirty t-shirts, your old shoes. If those same things were handed to you, you would have to squint your eyes to take them. Yet that's what you give to Allah ﷻ. This is the mentality being challenged here.

Allah ﷻ is talking about the roots of shirk. The root of shirk is that you are the priority, God isn't. So you can say whatever about God, make whatever up about God, because you come first.

translation: Or did We create the angels as females while they were witnesses
commentary:
أَمْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ إِنَـٰثًا وَهُمْ شَـٰهِدُونَ

"Or is it the case that We created the angels as females" - because you call them feminine, you call them daughters - وَهُمْ شَاهِدُونَ "and they were witnesses to that?"

translation: Unquestionably, it is out of their [invented] falsehood that they say
commentary:
أَلَآ إِنَّهُم مِّنْ إِفْكِهِمْ لَيَقُولُونَ

"You had better know - it is out of this vicious lie that they built, out of their إِفْك - their fabrication..."

translation: Allah has begotten," and indeed, they are liars
commentary:
وَلَدَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ

"...that they have the audacity to say 'Allah ﷻ gave birth,' or 'Allah ﷻ begat, fathered.'" وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَاذِبُونَ "And they, no doubt, are liars."

translation: Has He chosen daughters over sons
commentary:
أَصْطَفَى ٱلْبَنَاتِ عَلَى ٱلْبَنِينَ

"Did He choose daughters over sons?"

translation: What is [wrong] with you? How do you make judgement
commentary:
مَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ

"What is wrong with you? How do you make your decisions? How do you reach conclusions?"

translation: Then will you not be reminded
commentary:
أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ

"Don't you make any effort to take in some reminder, to engage in some remembrance?"

translation: Or do you have a clear authority
commentary:
أَمْ لَكُمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ مُّبِينٌ

"Or is it the case that you have an obvious, clear, authoritative proof - سُلْطَانٌ مُّبِينٌ - that gives you the right to say what you say?"

translation: Then produce your scripture, if you should be truthful
commentary:
فَأْتُوا۟ بِكِتَـٰبِكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

"Then fine - bring your book forward إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ- if in fact you're truthful."

translation: And they have claimed between Him and the jinn a lineage, but the jinn have already known that they [who made such claims] will be brought to [punishment]
commentary:
وَجَعَلُوا۟ بَيْنَهُۥ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ نَسَبًا ۚ وَلَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ ٱلْجِنَّةُ إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ

"And they even made relationships between Him and the jinn." نَسَبًا means to have a relationship, a kinship. Some of them even attributed to the jinn to be the children of Allah. وَلَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ الْجِنَّةُ "While the jinn already know very well" إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ "that they will be from those who are made to stand in trial." We've seen that scene already in this surah - everyone standing for judgment.

translation: Exalted is Allah above what they describe
commentary:
سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

"Declare Allah's perfection above and beyond all the descriptions, all the characterizations, all the mythologies that they build."

translation: Except the chosen servants of Allah [who do not share in that sin]
commentary:
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

"With the exception of the slaves of Allah ﷻ who have been granted sincerity" - المُخْلَصِينَ.

## Ayah 161-162

فَإِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ • مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ بِفَـٰتِنِينَ

"Then all of you and all the stuff that you worship - مَا أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ بِفَاتِنِينَ - you are not going to be able to put him in a test." You're not going to be able to throw the Prophet ﷺ off, and you're not going to be able to put anyone to the test.

translation: So indeed, you [disbelievers] and whatever you worship
You cannot tempt [anyone] away from Him
Except he who is to [enter and] burn in the Hellfire
commentary:
إِلَّا مَنْ هُوَ صَالِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

"Except the one who is already heading into the Jaheem" - the roaring flame anyway. What this means is: you and your gods are not going to be able to throw anybody off the religion except the people that are already meant for hellfire.

translation: [The angels say], "There is not among us any except that he has a known position
commentary:
وَمَا مِنَّآ إِلَّا لَهُۥ مَقَامٌ مَّعْلُومٌ

Now the angels are speaking at the end of the surah. The angels are the ones who were called "the daughters of Allah ﷻ," and now they speak directly. They say: "And we have no place at all. There's nothing that we have for ourselves at all except that He has assigned us مَقَامٌ مَّعْلُومٌ - a known place." We don't have anything of our own. He owns our known place, our known station, our known position, our known rank. We have to stand in rows. I belong in row one, another in row two, another in row three, another in row five hundred. We know our place, and He assigned that place.

translation: And indeed, we are those who line up [for prayer]
commentary:
وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلصَّآفُّونَ

"And we truly are الصَّافُّونَ - the ones ranked up in rows." The army itself is now speaking - we are the organized ranks.

translation: And indeed, we are those who exalt Allah
commentary:
وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْمُسَبِّحُونَ

"And we are truly the ones, المُسَبِّحُونَ, who are always declaring Allah's perfection." Which means we declare that Allah ﷻ is above all of these attributions, all these allegations.

## Ayah 167-168

وَإِن كَانُوا۟ لَيَقُولُونَ • لَوْ أَنَّ عِندَنَا ذِكْرًا مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

"And even if they're going to keep saying what they're saying" - and what did the criminals say in addition? "If we only had any kind of remembrance, any kind of revelation, just like the revelations from the old times - those old revelations - if they survived and we saw them..."

translation: And indeed, the disbelievers used to say
If we had a message from [those of] the former peoples
We would have been the chosen servants of Allah
commentary:
لَكُنَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

"...we would have turned into righteous slaves of Allah ﷻ as well."

translation: But they disbelieved in it, so they are going to know
commentary:
فَكَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ

"Then they disbelieved in that remembrance that has come to them. فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ - Soon they'll find out the consequences."

translation: And Our word has already preceded for Our servants, the messengers
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"And Our verdict has already come to pass. Our word has already been issued لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ - in the matter of Our slaves who were sent" - meaning Our slaves who are messengers.

translation: [That] indeed, they would be those given victory
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ ٱلْمَنصُورُونَ

إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ - "They are in fact the only ones, and the special ones, who are going to get the help."

This ayah teaches us something important about the difference between رَسُول and نَبِيّ - messenger and prophet. Allah ﷻ says the messengers - الرُّسُل - have to be aided. They will absolutely be aided in this world. They are not like the prophets mentioned elsewhere. Allah ﷻ says: كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي "Allah ﷻ has decreed, mandated, that I and My messengers must dominate. We will dominate." Prophets were sometimes killed, but messengers are aided by Allah ﷻ every time. This is a key distinction in understanding the roles and divine protection granted to those chosen to deliver Allah's message.

translation: And [that] indeed, Our soldiers will be those who overcome
commentary:
وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ

"And indeed, Our soldiers—they are the dominant ones."

Allah ﷻ reinforces His promise with emphasis: His armies will always prevail. The Arabic construction here is powerful—"Our armies, *they* are the ones who will dominate." This isn't merely about physical armies; it's about the forces Allah ﷻ mobilizes in support of His truth. The surah itself began with imagery of rows upon rows of angels (As-Saffat), establishing that the Prophet ﷺ has celestial support on his side. This assurance was crucial for the Messenger ﷺ in moments when the opposition seemed overwhelming and his message was met with mockery and rejection.

translation: So, [O Muhammad], leave them for a time
commentary:
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

"So turn away from them for a time."

Having established the certainty of victory, Allah ﷻ now gives the Prophet ﷺ practical guidance: don't let their ridicule affect you. When people refuse to take the message seriously, when they mock and dismiss it, you're instructed to simply turn away. This isn't abandonment of the mission—it's strategic patience. "Until a time" indicates that their period of respite is temporary and measured. The command to turn away is a mercy to the Messenger ﷺ: don't burden yourself with worry over those who reject you. Victory is already assured. You have an army—rows upon rows of support—so their mockery should not weigh heavily on your heart.

translation: And see [what will befall] them, for they are going to see
commentary:
وَأَبْصِرْهُمْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ

"And watch them, for they will soon see."

While turning away emotionally from their mockery, the Prophet ﷺ is told to remain observant. "Keep watch on them"—witness what unfolds. The contrast is striking: watch them (*absir*), because soon *they* will see (*yubsirun*). What they will see is the fulfillment of what they denied. The believers observe with the insight of faith, while the disbelievers will eventually see with the shock of reality catching up to them.

translation: Then for Our punishment are they impatient
commentary:
أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ

"Is it then Our punishment that they wish to hasten?"

This ayah addresses a recurring taunt from the disbelievers of Makkah. In their arrogance, they would mockingly challenge: "If you're truly a messenger, bring this punishment you keep warning us about!" They treated divine warnings as jokes, daring the punishment to come quickly. Allah ﷻ responds with a rhetorical question that highlights their foolishness: are they truly rushing toward their own destruction? Do they actually want Our punishment to arrive sooner?

translation: But when it descends in their territory, then evil is the morning of those who were warned
commentary:
فَإِذَا نَزَلَ بِسَاحَتِهِمْ فَسَآءَ صَبَاحُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ

"But when it descends in their courtyard, how terrible will be the morning of those who were warned."

The imagery here is vivid and striking. The word *saaha* refers to a courtyard or the open area right in front of a home—the most immediate, inescapable proximity. When punishment finally arrives, it won't be distant or abstract. It will drop right in their courtyard, at their very doorstep. The phrase "what a terrible morning" captures the sudden horror of awakening to a reality they had mocked. For those who were repeatedly warned (*mundharin*)—who had every opportunity to heed the message—that morning will be devastating. They rushed toward it with their taunts, and when it arrives, there will be no escape.

translation: And leave them for a time
commentary:
وَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

"And turn away from them for a time."

The instruction is repeated—turn away from them until the appointed time. This repetition serves as emphasis and reassurance. The first time this command appeared, it followed the promise that messengers are always aided and that Allah's ﷻ armies always prevail. Now it's repeated after describing the inevitable arrival of punishment. The message to the Prophet ﷺ is consistent: don't let their behavior disturb you. Your role is to deliver the message. The outcome is already determined by Allah ﷻ.

translation: And see, for they are going to see
commentary:
وَأَبْصِرْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ

"And watch, for they will soon see."

This too is repeated verbatim. The dual emphasis—both on turning away and on watching—creates a powerful psychological framework for the Prophet ﷺ. Maintain your emotional distance from their rejection, but remain observant of how events unfold. They will see the truth of what they denied, and you will witness their realization.

translation: Exalted is your Lord, the Lord of might, above what they describe
commentary:
سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ ٱلْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

"Glorified is your Master, the Master of Honor, above all that they describe."

The surah moves toward its conclusion with an act of *tasbih*—declaring Allah's ﷻ absolute perfection and transcendence. Your Master (*Rabbika*)—the One who nurtures and sustains you—is the Master of all *'izzah* (honor, dignity, might, authority). This is particularly meaningful in context: when the disbelievers mock the message and try to strip away the dignity of the Messenger ﷺ, they're powerless to do so. True honor belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ, and He grants it to whom He wills. Their attempts at humiliation are meaningless because they don't own dignity—Allah ﷻ does.

"Above all that they describe"—whatever characterizations they make of Allah ﷻ, whatever false descriptions they attribute to Him, He is infinitely beyond and above all of it. His perfection transcends their limited, flawed conceptions.

translation: And peace upon the messengers
commentary:
وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَى ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"And peace be upon the messengers."

This ayah beautifully encapsulates the core message of the entire surah. Throughout As-Saffat, we've encountered the stories of multiple messengers: Ibrahim (AS), Musa (AS), Harun (AS), Ilyas (AS), Lut (AS), Nuh (AS), and Yunus (AS). The surah even referenced the collective messengers with "Peace be upon the family of Yasin." Now, at the conclusion, a universal salutation: peace upon *all* the messengers—every single one who was ever sent.

The contrast is profound: nations are destroyed, but messengers are honored with peace. Civilizations rise and fall, empires crumble, but the legacy of the messengers endures. They may face persecution in their lifetimes, but their ultimate station is one of eternal peace and honor.

translation: And praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And all praise belongs to Allah, the Master of all the worlds."

The surah concludes with the same phrase that begins Surah Al-Fatihah: *Alhamdulillah*. All gratitude, all praise, all thanks belong exclusively to Allah ﷻ, the Master of *al-'alamin*—all peoples, all nations, all worlds, all of creation.

This ending carries deep significance. We've seen in the Quran that Surah Al-Fatihah and Surah Saba both begin with *Alhamdulillah*. We've seen surahs like Al-Isra that begin with declaring Allah's ﷻ perfection (*subhan*). Now we encounter a surah that ends with *Alhamdulillah*. 

The placement is meaningful: nations may be destroyed, and when every nation has passed away, when no civilization remains on earth, what endures? *Alhamdulillah*. Praise still belongs to Allah ﷻ. He is the Master of all worlds—those that exist now, those that existed before, and those yet to come. His dominion and His praise are eternal, transcending the rise and fall of human history.
